title: Phase 5: Multi-Omics Integration
text: Integrated RNA-seq expression, ATAC-seq accessibility, and pharmacogenomic scores using MOFA2 (unsupervised) and DIABLO (supervised) to identify coordinated multi-omic signatures of metabolizer status.

title: Phase 5: Multi-Omics Integration
text: Integrated RNA-seq expression, ATAC-seq accessibility, and pharmacogenomic scores using MOFA2 (unsupervised) and DIABLO (supervised) to identify coordinated multi-omic signatures of metabolizer status.

title: Phase 5: Multi-Omics Integration
text:
Key Components

MOFA2: Unsupervised factor discovery across RNA + ATAC modalities
DIABLO: Supervised integration using CYP activity scores as outcome labels
Input: DESeq2-normalized expression matrix, ATAC peak accessibility matrix, CYP2D6/CYP2C19 continuous scores
Goal: Identify latent factors that capture variance explained by pharmacogenomic stratification

title: Phase 5: Multi-Omics Integration
text:
Methods
MOFA2 trained on combined RNA + ATAC matrices with 10 latent factors. Assessed factor-PGx correlation via Spearman’s ρ. DIABLO trained with multi-block PLS-DA using continuous CYP scores as Y. Extracted loading weights to identify top contributing genes and peaks per component. Cross-validation via 5-fold CV to assess generalization.

title: Phase 5: Multi-Omics Integration
text:
Deliverables

MOFA2/DIABLO scripts
Factor loading tables (CSV): genes and peaks with top weights
Factor-PGx correlation heatmaps (PNG)
Component plots showing sample separation by metabolizer score
Variance explained per modality (bar plots)

title: Phase 5: Multi-Omics Integration
text:
Results
(Add figures when complete)

MOFA2 factor-PGx correlation matrix (heatmap)
Scatterplot of MOFA2 Factor 1 vs. CYP2D6 score
DIABLO component 1 vs. component 2 scatterplot colored by CYP tertiles
Top 20 genes/peaks per significant factor (bar plots of loadings)

title: Phase 4: Chromatin Accessibility
text: Analyzed single-nucleus ATAC-seq data from postmortem orbitofrontal cortex to identify open chromatin regions that correlate with CYP-associated gene expression patterns identified in Phase 2.

title: Phase 4: Chromatin Accessibility
text: Analyzed single-nucleus ATAC-seq data from postmortem orbitofrontal cortex to identify open chromatin regions that correlate with CYP-associated gene expression patterns identified in Phase 2.

title: Phase 4: Chromatin Accessibility
text:
Key Components

Input data: GEO GSE256207 (snATAC-seq, postmortem OFC, n=92 samples)
Preprocessing: ArchR or Signac pipeline (fragment deduplication, TSS enrichment QC, peak calling)
Peak-to-gene linkage: Cicero or ArchR P2G correlations
Integration strategy: Cross-cohort analysis linking ATAC peaks to Phase 2 DEGs via promoter/enhancer annotations

title: Phase 4: Chromatin Accessibility
text:
Methods
Standard snATAC-seq preprocessing with MACS2 peak calling. Used chromVAR for TF motif enrichment in differentially accessible regions. Since ATAC and RNA-seq are from separate cohorts, integration performed at the gene/region level rather than sample-level (standard cross-cohort approach). Focused on promoter regions (±2kb TSS) and distal regulatory elements linked to DEGs.

title: Phase 4: Chromatin Accessibility
text:
Deliverables

ATAC-seq analysis scripts
Differentially accessible peaks (BED files)
TF motif enrichment tables (CSV)
Peak-to-gene linkage tables (CSV)
UMAP visualizations colored by cell type and accessibility patterns

title: Phase 4: Chromatin Accessibility
text:

(Add figures when complete)

UMAP of snATAC-seq cells colored by cell type (excitatory, inhibitory, glia)
Heatmap of differentially accessible peaks near CYP-associated DEGs
TF motif enrichment bar plots (top 10 motifs)
Genome browser tracks showing example loci (e.g., key DEGs with nearby ATAC peaks)

title: Projects
text:
Pharmacogenomic analysis of psychiatric brain transcriptomics using fully open-access data. This project integrates SNP-derived CYP2D6/CYP2C19 activity scores with multi-region RNA-seq and postmortem scATAC-seq data.
Demonstrates: End-to-end multi-omics pipeline execution using only open-access resources — from raw data to interpretable biological insights.
Methods: PharmCAT · DESeq2 · MOFA2 · DIABLO · XGBoost · SHAP · PyTorch · Docker
Full Code: GitHub










CYP2D6/CYP2C19 star allele calling from SMRI SNP arrays using PharmCAT, generating continuous activity scores for 105 brains.












Multi-region brain RNA-seq differential expression analysis stratified by CYP2D6/CYP2C19 activity using DESeq2 across 7 brain regions.












Single-nucleus ATAC-seq analysis from postmortem OFC (GEO GSE256207) identifying pharmacogenomically-associated regulatory regions.












Unsupervised integration of RNA-seq, ATAC-seq, and pharmacogenomic data using MOFA2 and DIABLO to discover latent metabolizer-associated patterns.












XGBoost + SHAP models predicting CYP metabolizer status from multi-omics, with VAE benchmark and LLM-based interpretation pipeline.














Docker container with Bioconductor, Python, and all dependencies. Reproducible computational environment for the full pipeline.







No matching items

title: About Me
text:
My Journey
My interest in bioinformatics emerged from a fascination with how computational approaches can decode biological complexity. With a foundation in biomedical sciences and genomics, I’m now focused on building strong programming and data analysis skills to bridge wet-lab biology and computational research.
I’m working through bioinformatics problem sets, building analysis pipelines, and documenting my learning journey through this portfolio. My background includes teaching experience in gymnastics and education, which has strengthened my ability to break down complex concepts and communicate effectively.

title: About Me
text:
Interests Beyond Science


Scuba Diving
Open-water and nitrox certified. Diving demands methodical preparation, situational awareness, and staying calm under pressure — skills that translate directly to research and problem solving.


Martial Arts
Black belt in Taekwondo, with training in Muay Thai and Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu. Martial arts taught me that mastery is a process, not a destination — a philosophy I carry into learning bioinformatics.


Yu-Gi-Oh
Competitive player focused on deck building and strategic play. The game demands pattern recognition, probabilistic thinking, and adapting under pressure.


Writing
Currently writing Useless Man: Embracing the Exiled Self — a philosophical exploration of identity, purpose, and the freedom found in not fitting the expected mold.

title: About Me
text:
What’s Next
I’m actively seeking opportunities in bioinformatics, computational biology, or data science where I can apply my growing technical skills and contribute to meaningful research. I’m particularly interested in genomics, algorithm development, and data-driven biological discovery.
Open to: Full-time positions, internships, collaborations, and freelance projects

title: Research
text: Undergraduate research grounded in neuroscience, molecular biology and bioinformatics. My work focuses on applying quantitative methods to biological questions — connecting wet-lab context with data-driven analysis.

title: Research
text:
Selected Undergraduate Research

Digitization, Tracing, and Quantification of CGRP-IR Axons in the Nociceptive Cardiac Nervous System

Role: Undergraduate Researcher
Advisor: Dr. Zixi Cheng · Department of Biomedical Sciences · University of Central Florida
Duration: June 2022 – May 2023
Presentation: Student Scholar Symposium · University of Central Florida · March 28, 2023


This study used C57BL/6J mice which were sacrificed and perfused through the left ventricle with 4°C Zamboni’s fixative, the heart was then harvested and dissected into the right and left atria and ventricles. The samples were immunohistochemically (IHC) stained for calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP). The tissues were then prepared as flat-mounts and scanned using automated color fluorescence and confocal microscopy. The samples that were scanned for CGRP-IR axons – at 488 nm – were digitized and traced using a 3D neuron reconstruction software and finally quantified using ImageJ2 (Fiji). Within CGRP-IR axons, 2D and 3D digitalized axonal tracings have not been reported in the whole mouse heart, as a result, this data expanded that very limited dataset.

Download Poster (PDF)
Methods & Tools
Fluorescence/Confocal Microscopy · Neurolucida · ImageJ · GraphPad Prism · Microsoft Word/Excel · Adobe Photoshop/Illustrator · BioRendeer



Mutant U1A-RNA Binding Kinetics with Surface Plasmon Resonance

Role: Undergraduate Researcher
Advisor: Dr. Robert Borgon · Department of Biomedical Sciences · University of Central Florida
Duration: August 2022 – May 2023
Presentation: Amy Zeh High Impact Practice Student Showcase · University of Central Florida · April 19, 2023


This study involved the use of U1A which is a protein subunit that is part of the U1 snRNP and binds to U1 hairpin II (U1hpII) of the snRNA that associates with the U1 snRNP. The U1A wildtype is compared to point mutations incorporated into the mutated protein to find amino acids residues that have significance in binding. The binding efficacy is measured through kinetic data produced via surface plasmon resonance (SPR) which flows U1hpII over a NTA chip that has a ligand (i.e., U1A wildtype or mutants) immobilized to it via a His-tag. The produced binding curves will show the association and disassociation phases in which a mathematical model was applied to obtain the relevant kinetic constants (i.e., ka, kd, kDa). Furthermore, a proteomic analysis was done comparing the RNA recognition motifs (RRMs) of both U1A mutants and another critical subunit in the U1 snRNP.

Download Poster (PDF)
Methods & Tools
Surface Plasmon Resonance · PCR · Bacterial Cell Culture · R (programming language) · TraceDrawer · Microsoft Word/Excel/Powerpoint · GIMP · GraphPad Prism · BioRender


Microbiome gDNA analysis of non-infected and infected zombie ants using Illumina sequencing

Role: Undergraduate Student in Research-intensive Course
Professor: Dr. Anna Forsman · Genomics & Bioinformatics Cluster · University of Central Florida
Duration: January 2022 – May 2023


This study consisted of extracting and analyzing gDNA from the inside of ant gut microbiomes. The ants of interest are known as “Zombie Ants” because of a unique disease they contract from a pathogenic fungus called Ophiocordyceps that causes them to change their behavior and commit suicide so to promote more growth of the fungi. The objective was to find if there are any differences between the gut microbiome of ants infected with Ophiocordyceps (a fungus that manipulates then kills), Beauveria (a fungus that simply kills) or a normal ant that was treated with a placebo. The gDNA was extracted from the ants using a Microprep Kit then ran through a two-step PCR protocol to amplify the extracted gDNA. Next, a SpeedBead cleanup protocol was run to separate the loose primers/small fragments from the amplified gDNA to increase sequencing data quality. Finally, sequencing was performed using an in-house Illumina platform and the data was analyzed using QIIME 2 to cross-reference with BLAST in order to determine the genetic diversity present.

Methods & Tools
Illumina Sequencing · PCR · QIIME · Agarose Gel Electrophoresis · Microsoft Word/Excel

title: Research
text:
Research Interests
My primary interests are in neuropharmacogenomics pertaining to the expression of neurodegenerative diseases and mental health disorders. In particular, sequence analysis, variant interpretation, and building reproducible pipelines for high-throughput biological data is a major focus of mine. I’m drawn to work at the intersection of algorithmic problem-solving and biological discovery, and I’m actively developing the computational foundation to contribute to that kind of research.

title: Phase 3: RNA-seq Analysis
text: Performed differential gene expression analysis on SMRI Stanley RNA-seq data (7 brain regions) stratified by pharmacogenomic activity scores, identifying metabolizer-associated transcriptomic signatures.

title: Phase 3: RNA-seq Analysis
text: Performed differential gene expression analysis on SMRI Stanley RNA-seq data (7 brain regions) stratified by pharmacogenomic activity scores, identifying metabolizer-associated transcriptomic signatures.

title: Phase 3: RNA-seq Analysis
text:
Key Components

Input data: SMRI Stanley RNA-seq (ACC, DLPFC, NAc, Amy, Hipp, subCC, mOFC; n=60-105 per region)
Preprocessing: Read alignment (STAR), quantification (featureCounts), normalization (DESeq2 size factors)
Stratification: Correlation-based analysis using continuous CYP2D6/CYP2C19 scores as covariates
Differential expression: DESeq2 LRT and Wald tests controlling for diagnosis, age, sex, PMI, pH

title: Phase 3: RNA-seq Analysis
text:
Methods
DESeq2 design: ~ diagnosis + age + sex + PMI + pH + CYP2D6_score + CYP2C19_score. Used likelihood ratio test to identify genes where pharmacogenomic covariates improve model fit. FDR-corrected p < 0.05 threshold. Pathway enrichment via clusterProfiler (GO, KEGG, Reactome).

title: Phase 3: RNA-seq Analysis
text:
Deliverables

DESeq2 analysis scripts
DEG tables per region (CSV): DEGs_[region]_CYP2D6.csv, DEGs_[region]_CYP2C19.csv
Volcano plots and heatmaps (PNG)
Pathway enrichment tables (CSV)
Region-specific and shared DEG Venn diagrams

title: Phase 3: RNA-seq Analysis
text:
Results
(Add figures when complete)

Volcano plots showing DEGs for each region × enzyme combination
Heatmap of top 50 DEGs clustered by metabolizer score
Pathway enrichment bar plots (top 10 terms per comparison)
Cross-region overlap analysis (Venn diagrams)

title: Phase 6: Predictive Modeling & Interpretation
text: Built supervised gradient boosting models (XGBoost) to predict continuous CYP activity scores from RNA-seq and ATAC-seq features, using SHAP for biological interpretation. Benchmarked against a PyTorch VAE and deployed an LLM agent for automated result interpretation.

title: Phase 6: Predictive Modeling & Interpretation
text: Built supervised gradient boosting models (XGBoost) to predict continuous CYP activity scores from RNA-seq and ATAC-seq features, using SHAP for biological interpretation. Benchmarked against a PyTorch VAE and deployed an LLM agent for automated result interpretation.

title: Phase 6: Predictive Modeling & Interpretation
text:
Key Components

XGBoost model: Predict CYP2D6/CYP2C19 scores from top 500 genes + 500 peaks
SHAP analysis: Feature importance and interaction plots to identify key predictors
VAE benchmark: Compare unsupervised deep learning vs. classical integration methods
LLM agent: Automated interpretation pipeline with PubMed validation to prevent hallucination

title: Phase 6: Predictive Modeling & Interpretation
text:
Methods
XGBoost trained with 5-fold CV, hyperparameters tuned via Optuna. SHAP TreeExplainer used to compute feature importance (global) and dependence plots (local). VAE (2-layer encoder/decoder) trained on same multi-omic matrix; latent space correlation with CYP scores assessed. LLM agent (OpenAI GPT-4) queries PubMed for top SHAP features, validates biological plausibility, flags low-confidence claims.

title: Phase 6: Predictive Modeling & Interpretation
text:
Deliverables

XGBoost + SHAP scripts
VAE implementation (PyTorch)
LLM agent pipeline
Model performance tables (CSV): R², MAE per fold
SHAP summary plots (PNG): top 20 features globally
SHAP dependence plots (PNG): gene expression vs. SHAP value for top features
VAE latent space scatter plots vs. CYP scores
LLM interpretation report (Markdown) with PubMed citations

title: Phase 6: Predictive Modeling & Interpretation
text:
Results
(Add figures when complete)

XGBoost performance: predicted vs. actual CYP scores (scatterplot with R²)
SHAP summary plot (beeswarm) showing top predictive features
SHAP dependence plot for top gene (e.g., gene X expression vs. SHAP value)
VAE latent dimension correlation with CYP scores (heatmap)
LLM-generated interpretation excerpt with PubMed validation flags

title: Phase 2: Pharmacogenomic Genotyping
text: Extracted pharmacogenomic variants from SMRI Stanley Array Collection SNP data and computed continuous CYP2D6/CYP2C19 activity scores using PharmCAT and established pharmacogenetic translation tables.

title: Phase 2: Pharmacogenomic Genotyping
text: Extracted pharmacogenomic variants from SMRI Stanley Array Collection SNP data and computed continuous CYP2D6/CYP2C19 activity scores using PharmCAT and established pharmacogenetic translation tables.

title: Phase 2: Pharmacogenomic Genotyping
text:
Key Components

Input data: SMRI Stanley Array Collection (n=105 postmortem brains, ~900K SNPs)
Variant extraction: Isolated CYP2D6 (22q13.2), CYP2C19 (10q23.33) loci from SNP arrays
Star allele calling: PharmCAT haplotype inference from genotype data
Activity scoring: Continuous metabolizer scores (0.0-3.0 scale) instead of categorical bins

title: Phase 2: Pharmacogenomic Genotyping
text:
Methods
Used PharmCAT v2.x pipeline for automated star allele calling from VCF-formatted SNP array data. Activity scores computed as sum of allele function values (e.g., 1/1 = 2.0, 1/4 = 1.0, 4/4 = 0.0 for loss-of-function alleles). Continuous scoring preserves statistical power vs. traditional categorical metabolizer bins (PM/IM/EM/UM).

title: Phase 2: Pharmacogenomic Genotyping
text:
Deliverables

PharmCAT pipeline script
Activity score table (CSV): pgx_activity_scores.csv
Star allele distribution plots (PNG)
Quality control report: genotype call rates, allele frequency validation

title: Phase 2: Pharmacogenomic Genotyping
text:
Results
(Add figures when complete)

Distribution histogram of CYP2D6/CYP2C19 continuous scores
Correlation plot between the two enzymes
Comparison to published allele frequencies (validation check)
